name: Earl May
abstract: Earl Charles Barrington May (September 17, 1927 - January 4, 2008) was an American jazz bassist. Allmusic described him as "one of the most prodigious and prolific bassists of the postwar era".

interviews.title: Earl May interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 11, 2001

concerts.label: WLIB-FM Festival of Music and Drama
Newport Jazz Festival: Dizzy Gillespie Quartet and Freddie Hubbard Quintet

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: My first, really when I began to support a trio and began to get out there with a trio, the trio was Earl May and Charlie Smith. And we worked together for a long time.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.evidence: I have a seven piece group called The Dukes... and guys like Earl May on bass, these things like that, so it’s a great band.

about_quotes.speaker: Junior Mance Part II
about_quotes.evidence: Earl May has been sort of our regular bass player. ... That’s usually the trio I use most of the time now
about_quotes.doc_title: Junior Mance Part II interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 18, 1999 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Al Gafa
about_quotes.evidence: We brought in Larry Willis on piano, Earl May on drums... This group was dynamite.
about_quotes.doc_title: Al Gafa interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 2, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: The bassist, believe it or not, is a sub for the regular bassist, who’s Earl May... That’s it. We have a couple of vocalists that we like to work with. ... That’s the Loud Minority family.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Ascione
about_quotes.evidence: Selden Powell, Earl May, McKenna. And it was like what is this, two, three drummers? I couldn’t understand what was going on.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Ascione interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 17, 2001
